ECB SAFE Survey — Wave 37

Slovakia · Euro Area · Germany  |  Generated 30 Jun 2026

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Slovak firms reported a sharp easing in bank loan pricing, with a net 8.3% experiencing lower interest rates, contrasting with tightening non-interest costs and loan sizes, signalling uneven improvements in financing conditions.
  • Financing gaps improved across instruments, yet trade credit remained the most stressed, with demand-driven pressure pushing the gap to +9.8pp, while bank loans turned negative at -7.6pp, easing Slovak firms’ stress relative to the euro area.
  • Slovak firms faced a persistent cost squeeze, with labour and input costs rising sharply (61.84% and 57.71% net balances, respectively), while turnover and profitability declined, compounding financial strain despite some easing in macroeconomic pessimism.
  • Business outlook softened, with turnover expectations turning less optimistic and fixed investment plans falling sharply, though inflation and price expectations remained elevated, reflecting ongoing cost pressures and weak demand dynamics.
  • Access to finance was the least pressing business obstacle for Slovak firms (3.72/10), overshadowed by labour shortages (6.57/10), while trade credit availability worsened further, highlighting structural vulnerabilities in non-bank financing.

Financing Conditions

Slovak firms report net easing in interest rates, but rising non-interest costs

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net -8.3% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported net easing in interest rate levels, a notable improvement from a net -25.2% in the previous wave, contrasting with net tightening of +11.1% across the EA.
  • A net 42.4% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported tightening in non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions), up sharply from n/a in wave 36 and well above the EA net balance of +28.4% (n=1,762).
  • A net 7.0% of Slovak firms (n=48) reported tightening in available loan size, reversing the net easing of -7.9% in the prior wave, slightly above the EA net balance of +3.7% (n=1,748).

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak bank loan conditions ease sharply as financing need falls

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • The Slovak bank loan gap turned negative at -7.6pp (n=240), swinging from +10.5pp in the prior wave, as need fell sharply to -6.2pp (from +5.4pp) and availability turned positive (+1.4pp vs -5.1pp), leaving SK far less stressed than the EA (+3.7pp).
  • While bank loan conditions eased markedly, the credit line gap remained elevated at +4.8pp (n=256) — narrowing from +13.1pp but driven almost entirely by falling need (+3.1pp vs +8.8pp), with availability only marginally less tight (-1.7pp vs -4.3pp); SK is nonetheless less stressed than the EA (6.6pp).
  • Trade credit remained the most stressed instrument for Slovak firms, with the gap at +9.8pp (n=176) — narrowing from +16.4pp but still +3.0pp above the EA (+6.8pp), as need surged to +10.1pp while availability remained near zero (+0.3pp), signalling demand-driven pressure persists.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak financing gap halved as rejection rate fell sharply in wave 37

Bank Loan Applications and Access Conditions (Q7A/Q7B)

  • A net 3.58% of Slovak firms (n=147) faced a bank-credit financing gap in wave 37, well below the EA average of 5.02% (n=3,406) and a sharp improvement from 7.38% in the previous wave.
  • The improvement was driven by a fall in the rejection rate, from 8.78% to 5.31% among Slovak applicants, signalling a supply-side easing rather than a change in demand-side self-censorship.
  • Slovak discouragement remained contained at 2.34% in wave 37, below the EA's 3.22%, suggesting Slovak firms are not unusually deterred from applying for bank credit.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms' bank loan availability expectations improved sharply, nearing EA levels

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net -1.93% of Slovak firms (n=125) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate in wave 37, a marked improvement compared with a net -12.62% in the previous wave.
  • Slovak firms' forward expectations for bank loans nearly converged with the EA average (net -0.45%, n=2,688), suggesting local supply tightness is easing after a period of significant divergence.
  • Slovak firms remained notably more pessimistic on trade credit availability (net -11.68%, n=89) than the EA (net -0.45%), and worsened relative to EA peers who shifted from positive to marginally negative territory, signalling persistent strain in non-bank short-term financing.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms heavily favour inventory financing, far outpacing euro area peers

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • A notable 55.8% of Slovak firms (n=237) cited inventory and working capital as a financing purpose, well above the EA average of 38.8% and up from 53.2% in the previous wave.
  • Some 40.3% of Slovak firms (n=237) cited fixed investment, broadly in line with the EA (39.4%) and slightly up from 39.1% in wave 36, contrasting with DE's markedly higher 49.0%.
  • Refinancing or paying off obligations was cited by 20.6% of Slovak firms (n=236), a sharp rise from 14.7% in the previous quarter, surpassing the EA share of 14.3%.

Slovak firms report improved bank willingness to lend, outpacing euro-area peers

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net +10.4% of Slovak firms (n=300) reported improved willingness of banks to provide credit in wave 37, a sharp reversal from a net -2.4% in the previous wave, and above the EA net of +5.7%.
  • A net -38.3% of Slovak firms (n=460) reported a deterioration in the general economic outlook, signalling a persistent macro-level drag, though this improved markedly from a net -51.2% in wave 36.
  • A net -25.0% of Slovak firms (n=328) reported deteriorating access to public financial support in wave 37, compared with a net -33.9% previously, still worse than the EA average of -15.3%.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak firms report falling turnover while labour and other costs surge

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net -14.62% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported declining turnover in wave 37, barely improved from a net -15.16% in the previous quarter, and sharply diverging from the EA net of +5.91%.
  • A net 61.84% of Slovak firms reported rising labour costs — 11.6 percentage points above the EA average of 50.23% — and other costs also outpaced the EA (net +57.71% vs. +50.89%), intensifying the cost-squeeze on Slovak businesses.
  • Slovak firms' profitability remains deeply negative at a net -22.43% (n=237), but this marks a significant improvement from a net -37.52% in wave 36, compared with the EA net of -10.37%.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms remain cautiously optimistic but outlook softens across turnover and investment

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 20.90% of Slovak firms (n=169) reported expecting turnover to increase, compared with a net 24.18% in the previous wave, narrowing the gap with the EA (net +23.16%).
  • Slovak fixed investment expectations fell sharply, with a net 10.79% of firms (n=169) anticipating increases versus a net 25.70% in the prior wave, now lagging the EA average of net +12.58%.
  • Both SK and EA turnover outlooks softened in wave 36, but the EA decline was steeper (from +27.78% to +23.16%), suggesting broadly shared headwinds rather than a Slovakia-specific divergence.

Slovak firms expect input prices to surge nearly 8.4%, threatening margins

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • A mean expected selling price increase of +4.63% was reported by Slovak firms (n=226) in wave 37, well above the EA average of +2.77% and up from +3.41% in the previous wave.
  • Slovak firms anticipate +8.37% input price rises (n=230) alongside +5.49% wage growth, compressing margins — both readings sharply above EA peers (+3.53% and +3.11% respectively) — and consistent with a labour cost net balance of +61.84% in the current situation, the highest in the latest wave.
  • Short-run inflation expectations among Slovak firms rose to a mean of +4.79% (n=211) for the 12-month horizon, up from +4.34% in wave 36 and nearly 1.5 percentage points above the EA mean of +3.36%, signalling persistently elevated price pressures.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms see sharply higher upside inflation risks than euro-area peers

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 53% of Slovak firms (n=237) reported upside inflation risks in wave 37, compared with a net 61% in the previous wave, remaining well above the euro-area net of 45% (n=5,067).
  • Slovak firms' upside risk balance exceeded the EA by roughly 8 percentage points in wave 37, a persistent divergence that signals local price pressures are not fully converging with the broader euro area.
  • Corroborating this, Slovak firms reported average selling price expectations of 4.63% for the next year versus 2.77% for EA peers, while input price expectations stood at 8.37% in Slovakia compared with 3.53% in the EA.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms rate access to finance least pressing, well below EA average

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Slovak firms rated access to finance at 3.72 out of 10 (n=233) in wave 37, 0.42 points below the euro area average of 4.14, the widest SK-EA gap in the survey.
  • Access to finance remained virtually unchanged for Slovak firms compared with a score of 3.73 in the previous wave (wave 30), while the EA score edged up from 4.06 to 4.14.
  • The most pressing concern for Slovak firms was availability of skilled staff (6.57 out of 10, n=237), nearly three full points above the access-to-finance score of 3.72.

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
